Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION USING VARIATIONAL INFERENCE FOR BIOMEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Deep learning motivated by convolutional neural networks has been highly successful in a range of medical imaging problems like image classification, image segmentation, image synthesis etc. However for validation and interpretability, not only do we need the predictions made by the model but also how confident it is while making those predictions. This is important in safety critical applications for the people to accept it. In this work, we used an encoder decoder architecture based on variational inference techniques for segmenting brain tumour images. We evaluate our work on the publicly available BRATS dataset using Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and Intersection Over Union (IOU) as the evaluation metrics. Our model is able to segment brain tumours while taking into account both aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty in a principled bayesian manner.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Medical image segmentation is a challenging task for medical practitioners. It is costly, takes time and is prone to error. Hence there is a need to automate the manually done segmentation. Lately Neural Networks have shown great potential on a variety of medical image segmentation problems. The challenge with the approaches used in literature is that the model doesn’t predict the uncertainty associated. This is where Bayesian methods come into play as it gives a principled way of measuring uncertainty from the model predictions. Measuring uncertainty in the output predictions made by neural networks is important for interpretation and validation. Rather than learning the point estimates, Bayesian Neural Networks (BNN) learns the distribution over the weights. The training process of BNN involves first initializing the parameters of the neural network. Next the weights are sampled from some distribution (like gaussian with zero mean and unit variance) and both the forward pass and backward pass is done to update the weights using the conventional backpropagation algorithm.

Monte Carlo dropout networks (Kingma et al., 2015) use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes which still lack theoretical understanding. Bayesian Convolutional Neural Network (Gal and Ghahramani, 2015) use variational inference to learn the posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset. The problem with this approach is that it requires a lot of computation involving a lot of parameters, making this technique not scalable in practice. Variational Autoencoder (Kingma et al., 2015) which is based on generative models solves the above problems and has been successful in a number of tasks like generating images, texts, recommender systems etc. This approach comes with several challenges in its own right which have been successfully tackled in the literature. A random variable sampled from posterior distribution has no gradient so the conventional backpropagation techniques can’t be applied to it. Local Reparameterization Trick (Kingma et al., 2015) was proposed to tackle this by converting the random variable to a deterministic one for computation. The second challenge was the huge computational requirement since it required weight updates in every iteration. Bayes by Backprop algorithm (Blundell et al., 2015) tackled this by calculating gradients in backpropagation using a scale and shift approach by updating the posterior distribution in the backward pass.

## 2 RELATED WORK

## 2.1 MEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

The problem of segmenting medical images have been successfully tackled in literature using mainly two techniques, first using a Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) (Long et al., 2015) and second those which are based on U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015). The main characteristic of FCN architectures is that it doesn’t use fully connected layers at the end which have been used successfully for image classification problems. U-Net methods on the other hand uses an encoder-decoder architecture with pooling layers in the encoder and upsampling layers in the decoder. Skip connections connect the encoder layers to the decoder layer to create an additional path for the flow of gradients back in the backpropagation step. This helps in reducing overfitting due to many parameters involved while training the network.

## 2.2 BAYESIAN NEURAL NETWORK

Lately, there has been a revival of interest in bayesian methods as some of the inherent problems with deep learning could be solved using it. It is a scalable approach of avoiding overfitting in neural networks and at the same time gives us a measure of uncertainty. This is very important in critical applications where not only do we require the predictions made from the model, but also how confident it is while making its predictions. BNN can be considered as an ensemble of neural networks (Gal, 2016). It has two advantages over the standard neural networks, first it avoids overfitting and second it gives a measure of uncertainty involved.
Instead of point estimates, the neural network learns posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset as defined in Equation 1.
The predictive distribution can be calculated by approximating the integral as defined in Equation 2.

The challenge is that the posterior is often intractable in nature. To combat this, (Neal, 1993) used Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) for learning the weights over the bayesian neural networks. Also (Graves, 2011), (Blundell et al., 2015) and (Louizos and Welling, 2016) proposed independently a technique using variational inference techniques for approximating the posterior distribution. KL Divergence between the posterior and the true distribution can be calculated using Equation 3.
Alternatively minimizing the KL divergence can be written in another form by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) which is tractable. This is shown in Equation 4.

## 2.3 VARIATIONAL INFERENCE

Variational inference finds the parameters of the distribution by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound. ELBO consists of sum of two terms Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between two distributions and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) as defined in Equation 5.

The KL divergence is defined in equation 6.
The posterior in the above equation contains an integral which is intractable in nature. The equation can be re written in Equation 7.

The above equation can be decomposed into two parts one of which is the KL divergence between the exact posterior and its variational approximation which needs to be minimized and the second is ELBO term which needs to be maximized. This is shown in Equation 8.

KL divergence is zero if exact posterior is equal to variational approximation. Since the KL divergence is always greater than zero hence the equation can be approximated by maximizing only the ELBO (Kingma et al., 2015) as defined in equation 9.

## 2.4 ALEATORIC UNCERTAINTY AND EPISTEMIC UNCERTAINTY

There are two types of uncertainty - aleatory and epistemic uncertainty where variance is the sum of both these. Bayesian Neural Networks can be considered an ensemble of neural networks initialized randomly which averages the test results in parallel (Gal, 2016). For final predictions, single mean and variance can be estimated as shown in Equation 10 and Equation 11 respectively.

The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty. Bayesian Neural Network model for uncertainty estimation was done by (Kendall and Gal, 2017) with the last layer representing the mean and variance of logits. The predictive distribution approximating the posterior distribution which gives a measure of uncertainty is defined in Equation 12.

Aleatoric uncertainty is a measure of the variability of the predictions from the dataset hence it is inherent in the data present. The aleatoric uncertainty is the uncertainty that exists inside the dataset. It captures noise inherent in the observations, which arises from the distribution of data. It is highly dependent on bias and distribution inside the input data but not the size of training samples.

3

Epistemic uncertainty on the other hand is a measure of the variability of predictions from the model which is tied to various metrics used for evaluation like accuracy, loss etc. It represents the uncertainty inside the model. The epistemic uncertainty will decrease with enough training samples. It is resulting from the limitation of knowledge and data of the system.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 DATASET

To validate the performance of our proposed approach to generalization, publicly available datasets were used for brain tumour segmentation BRATS18 (Menze et al., 2015) and (Bakas et al., 2018). It contains MRI scans of 175 patients with glioblastoma and lower grade glioblastoma. The images were of resolution 240 _×_ 240 _×_ 155 pixels. The ground truth labels were created by expert neuroradiologists. The sample from the dataset is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 1: Example of MRI slices and ground truth segmentation

## 3.2 DATA AUGMENTATION

The following data augmentation methods were used to increase the size of dataset:

**1. Rescaling:** We rescale the pixels values by rescaling factor 1/255.

**2. Rotation:** Random rotations with the setup degree range between [0, 360] were used.
**3. Height and Width Shift:** Shifting the input to the left or right and up or down was performed.
**4. Shearing Intensity:** It refers to shear angle (unit in degrees) in counter-clockwise direction.

**5. Brightness:** It uses a brightness shift value from the setup range.

## 3.3 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

The prior distribution helps to incorporate learning of the weights over the network. Variational Autoencoder has been used successively as a kind of generative model by sampling from the prior distribution in the encoder. The decoder uses the mean vector and standard deviation vector from the latent space to reconstruct the input. Our model uses a similar encoder decoder architecture as that used in VAEs with the input to the encoder coming from a pre trained image segmentation architecture. We tried different backbones which have previously enjoyed success and found original U-Net gave the best results. The input to the encoder only needs the mean vector, the standard deviation vector of the conditional distribution expressing the confidence with which the pixels are correctly predicted. After passing through the encoder, the parameters get converted to a latent representation which is again sampled in a mean and standard deviation vector. The decoder later recovers this back to the original distribution. The conventional backpropagation algorithm is used for training the model with gradient descent. The network architecture is shown in Figure 1.

## 3.5 LOSS FUNCTIONS

A combination of binary cross entropy and dice losses have been used to train the network. The first part binary cross entropy is a commonly used loss function for classification problems as shown by (Goodfellow et al., 2016). Every pixel in the image needs to be classified and hence loss function can be written as shown in Equation 18.

The problem with binary cross entropy loss is that it doesn’t take into account the class imbalance as the background is the dominant class. This is one of fundamental challenges in semantic segmentation problems. Dice Loss is robust to the aforementioned problem which is based on Dice Similarity Coefficient as defined in Equation 19.
Both the loss terms were combined in a single term with more weight given to the Dice Loss term since it handles the class imbalance problem better. This is shown in Equation 20.

## 3.6 EVALUATION METRICS

Evaluation metrics for semantic segmentation problems which have often been used in literature are Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) also known as F1-score and Intersection over union (IoU). The corresponding equations are shown in Equation 16 and Equation 17 respectively.

True positive (TR), false negative (FN) and false positive (FP) number of pixels is calculated separately for each image and averaged over the test set. The ground truth is labelled manually by experts which are compared against.

## 4 RESULTS

Figure 3 displays how accuracy (y-axis) behaves based on how much the data (x-axis) holds.

The darker(dull) color denotes more confidence while the lighter(brighter) color means the model is less confident while segmenting those areas. Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we proposed a way to quantify uncertainty in the context of medical image segmentation. Our network is based on an encoder decoder framework similar to that used by VAEs. The weights of the network represent distributions instead of point estimates and thus give a principled way of measuring uncertainty at the same time while making the predictions. Our model uses bayesian neural networks for both the encoder and decoder. The inputs to encoder come from pre trained backbones like U-Net, V-Net and FCN sampled from conditional distribution representing the confidence with which pixels are labelled correctly. We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a principled Bayesian approach to uncertainty quantification in biomedical image segmentation using variational inference, with a focus on brain tumor segmentation via the BRATS dataset. While the topic is timely and relevant—especially given the critical need for reliable uncertainty estimates in clinical decision-making—the presentation suffers from several significant issues that affect clarity, technical rigor, and overall scientific contribution. The abstract and introduction suggest a novel integration of variational inference for capturing both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, but the methodological details are underdeveloped and at times confusing, particularly in the description of network architecture and training procedure. Key equations are referenced (e.g., Equation 1 through 9), yet the absence of actual mathematical expressions or sufficient verbal explanation impairs reproducibility and understanding. Furthermore, while the paper cites foundational works such as Gal & Ghahramani (2015) and Kendall & Gal (2017), it fails to clearly differentiate its approach from existing methods—especially in how it jointly models both types of uncertainty within a variational autoencoder-like framework—and does not adequately justify architectural choices. Basic concepts such as the reparameterization trick and ELBO are explained at a level suitable for an introductory tutorial, suggesting a lack of concision and focus for a conference venue like ICLR, where technical depth is expected. Evaluation is mentioned in terms of DSC and IoU, but without baseline comparisons or ablation on uncertainty calibration (e.g., via reliability diagrams or uncertainty-sensitivity analysis), claims about performance and robustness remain unsubstantiated. Most critically, the work currently lacks experimental validation of the uncertainty estimates themselves—measures such as predictive entropy, mutual information, or variance maps—which are essential for supporting the paper's central claims. To strengthen the paper, the authors must clarify the model formulation, provide rigorous experimental evaluation of uncertainty quality (not just segmentation accuracy), and better position their contribution relative to the state of the art in Bayesian deep learning for medical imaging.

comments.title: Incorrect decomposition claim in variational inference section
comments.quote: The above equation can be decomposed into two parts one of which is the KL divergence between the exact posterior and its variational approximation which needs to be minimized and the second is ELBO term which needs to be maximized. This is shown in Equation 8.
comments.explanation: The text claims that the KL divergence is between the 'exact posterior and its variational approximation', implying KL(p||q). However, in standard variational inference, the correct term is KL(q||p), which is used in the ELBO identity: log p(D) = ELBO + KL(q||p). Since KL divergence is not symmetric, this misstatement reflects a fundamental misunderstanding. The phrasing risks misleading readers about the foundations of VI, particularly how maximizing ELBO corresponds to minimizing KL(q||p). This conceptual error is not resolved in context and contradicts standard references (e.g., Bishop, PRML; Kingma & Welling, 2013).

comments.title: Confusing and potentially incorrect claim about variance decomposition in uncertainty types
comments.quote: There are two types of uncertainty - aleatory and epistemic uncertainty where variance is the sum of both these.
comments.explanation: The statement that 'variance is the sum' of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties is ambiguous and technically problematic. In regression, predictive variance can be decomposed as Var(y) = Ep[Var(y|w)] + Varp[E(y|w)], aligning with aleatoric and epistemic components. However, in segmentation (a classification task), uncertainty is typically measured via entropy or mutual information, not variance. Using 'variance' without clarification in this context is misleading. Even if applied in logit space, the lack of formal definition and justification for equating it with uncertainty decomposition undermines rigor. This issue is compounded by later claims of inverse correlation between the two uncertainties without evidence.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'aleatoric' vs. 'aleatory'
comments.quote: There are two types of uncertainty - aleatory and epistemic uncertainty
comments.explanation: The term 'aleatory' appears once while the rest of the paper consistently uses 'aleatoric'. While synonymous, 'aleatoric' is standard in machine learning literature (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017). The inconsistency may confuse readers unfamiliar with the terms. Given that surrounding sections use 'aleatoric', this appears to be a spelling error rather than a technical one. It does not affect correctness but harms clarity and professionalism.

comments.title: Unclear definition of encoder and decoder functions in algorithm setup
comments.quote: Let dataset be denoted by _D_ : _{_ ( _xi, yi_ ) _}[N] i_ =1[, variational approximation of the posterior distribution] by _qθ_ ( _w_ ), encoder as _rψ_ ( _z|w_ ) and decoder as _pφ_ ( _w_ ).
comments.explanation: The notation contradicts standard VAE conventions: the encoder is defined as rψ(z|w), conditioning on weights w, and the decoder as pφ(w) without conditioning on latent z. In typical VAEs, the encoder infers latent z from input, and the decoder reconstructs data from z. Here, reconstructing weights w from z via pφ(w|z) suggests the decoder should be conditional. Algorithm 1 uses pφ(w[i]|z), confirming that the text definition is underspecified. This inconsistency makes the architecture ambiguous and hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing minus sign in ELBO objective in Equation 5
comments.explanation: Equation 5 improperly presents the minimization of KL(q||p(w|D)) as the objective, but this term is intractable and not directly optimized. The correct approach is maximizing the ELBO, which includes a negative KL term: ELBO = E_q[log p(D,w)] - KL(q||p). The text incorrectly states that ELBO 'consists of sum of two terms: KL divergence and NLL', which is backwards—KL appears negatively. This misrepresentation suggests that higher KL improves the objective, which is false. While experts may infer the correction, it constitutes a significant conceptual flaw in exposition.

comments.title: Misattribution of Monte Carlo dropout paper
comments.quote: Monte Carlo dropout networks (Kingma et al., 2015) use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes
comments.explanation: The foundational work on Monte Carlo dropout for Bayesian inference is due to Gal and Ghahramani (2015, 2016), who showed that dropout approximates a Bernoulli variational inference. Kingma et al. (2015) introduced variational dropout with local reparameterization but did not propose MC dropout at test time. Citing Kingma et al. for MC dropout misattributes a key idea and undermines academic precision. Gal & Ghahramani are cited elsewhere, suggesting oversight. This does not invalidate the technical content but weakens scholarly credibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous decomposition of variance into aleatoric and epistemic components
comments.quote: The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The paper implies a direct variance decomposition into aleatoric and epistemic components via Equation 11: $ \hat{\sigma}^2 = \left(\frac{1}{T}\sum\sigma_t^2\right) + \left(\frac{1}{T}\sum\mu_t^2 - \hat{\mu}^2\right) $. While this heuristic is sometimes used, the text fails to clarify that it's post-hoc and model-dependent. Standard Bayesian practice separates uncertainties through explicit modeling: aleatoric via output distribution parameters, epistemic via weight sampling. By conflating post-processing with principled modeling, and without formal definition, the paper risks misrepresenting how uncertainty is fundamentally captured, especially given its emphasis on their relationship.

comments.title: Incorrect objective function in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Compute stochastic gradients of the objective:     _L_[approx] =[] _i_  _−_ log _qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][] _−_ log _rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][] + log _pφ_ ( _i_ )  _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _|z_
comments.explanation: The objective in Algorithm 1 lacks a data-likelihood term $ \log p(D|w) $, rendering it incompatible with supervised learning. Instead, it optimizes a generative modeling objective over weights and latents without linking to labels. For segmentation, this omission means the model cannot learn a meaningful posterior for the task. Although Eq 13 may include the missing term, its absence from Algorithm 1 makes the procedure irreproducible. This is a major theoretical flaw that invalidates the algorithm as presented.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed input source and network design
comments.quote: Our model uses a similar encoder decoder architecture as that used in VAEs with the input to the encoder coming from a pre trained image segmentation architecture.
comments.explanation: The claim that inputs come from a pretrained segmentation network (e.g., U-Net) contradicts standard practice where raw images are used. If the input were segmentation maps, it would require a different architecture. Additionally, the model is trained end-to-end, which conflicts with using fixed pretrained outputs. While feature distillation or multitask learning could justify such setup, the authors do not clarify this. The ambiguity affects reproducibility and raises questions about the model's actual input modality.

comments.title: Undefined reparameterization sampling and missing Monte Carlo averaging in prediction
comments.quote: Sample weights with reparametrization: _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][]  Sample latent variables with reparametrization: _z_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][]
comments.explanation: Although sampling is performed during training, the inference phase lacks a Monte Carlo sampling loop for uncertainty quantification. In Bayesian models, multiple forward passes are needed to estimate predictive variance. Algorithm 1 computes gradients but omits test-time sampling or aggregation details. Furthermore, Equation 10 and 11 are referenced for estimating mean and variance, but it is unclear whether both weight and latent samples contribute, and how they are combined. This underspecification undermines reproducibility and proper uncertainty estimation.

comments.title: Uncertainty Calculation in Deep Ensembles Uses Entropy of Averaged Predictions, Not Variance
comments.quote: **2. Deep ensembles:** We collect the results of each image trained on five models and compute the average mean. We then calculate the entropy of these mean values as the uncertainty value on each image.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'entropy of these mean values' is misleading—it suggests entropy is computed over scalar means rather than over a probability distribution. Correctly interpreted, they likely compute the mean class probabilities across models and then entropy of that distribution: $ H(\bar{p}) = -\sum_c \bar{p}_c \log \bar{p}_c $. While this is a valid method, the awkward wording risks misinterpretation. It may lead readers to believe uncertainty arises from variability in means rather than uncertainty in the prediction itself.

comments.title: Ensemble MC Dropout Equation Missing from Text
comments.quote:
This can be defined using the below equation:

where _D_ presents how many models are there in ensemble MC dropout, _k_ , _i_ , _j_ are the indexes of _D_ , _M_ , _c_ and other variables same as MC dropout uncertainty formula above.
comments.explanation: The text promises an equation for 'Ensemble MC Dropout' but fails to provide it. It only describes index variables and refers vaguely to prior formulas. The expected form should involve a double average over models and Monte Carlo samples. Without explicit definition—whether uncertainty is computed per-model or after averaging—it's impossible to assess reproducibility. This is especially critical given that Table 2 compares results across methods using potentially inconsistent measures.

comments.title: Table numbering conflict and mismatched content
comments.quote: The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2.
comments.explanation: There is a clear cross-reference error: the text refers to 'Table 2' for Mean Dice Similarity (DSC) values, but Table 2 actually reports uncertainty metrics. DSC values are in Table 3. Similarly, the text later refers to Table 3 for IoU, but IoU is in Table 4. This likely stems from the earlier uncertainty table being labeled Table 2. While tables contain correct data, this reference swap creates confusion about which results support which claims, especially for reproducibility and interpretation.

comments.title: Claimed Superiority to State-of-the-Art Lacks Supporting Evidence
comments.quote: We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.
comments.explanation: No comparison to prior work or published state-of-the-art models on BRATS is provided. Tables 3 and 4 only show performance across different training sizes, not against baseline models. The highest DSC (64.3 with 20 patients) is far below typical BRATS performance (>80), suggesting a limited data setting. Without benchmark comparisons or citation of baselines (e.g., 3D U-Net, prior uncertainty methods), the claim of outperforming SOTA is unjustified and potentially misleading.

comments.title: Aleatoric and Epistemic Uncertainties Described as Inversely Correlated Without Justification
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The claim of an inverse relationship between aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty contradicts standard understanding. These uncertainties capture distinct sources—data noise and model uncertainty—and are often positively correlated in ambiguous regions. While visual complementarity in uncertainty maps may suggest inverse patterns, no statistical analysis (e.g., pixel-wise correlation) supports a systematic inverse correlation. Presenting this as a general property, without evidence or qualification, misleads readers about the nature of uncertainty decomposition.

comments.title: Inconsistent ensemble MC dropout uncertainty formula in text vs. definition
comments.quote: Ensemble MC dropout uncertainty: $ \text{Uncertainty} = \frac{1}{D} \sum_{k=1}^D \left[ \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^M p_i^{(k,m)} (1 - p_i^{(k,m)}) \right] $
comments.explanation: The formula uses Bernoulli variance $ p(1-p) $ averaged over models and MC samples, but this conflates aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty without decomposition. Meanwhile, the entropy-based measure used for deep ensembles computes uncertainty from averaged predictions—methodologically different. Using incompatible uncertainty quantification across methods undermines the validity of comparisons in Table 2. The lack of reconciliation or justification for different approaches questions whether the observed 'higher uncertainty' in ensemble MC dropout reflects model behavior or metric design.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a principled Bayesian approach to uncertainty quantification in biomedical image segmentation using variational inference, with a focus on brain tumor segmentation via the BRATS dataset. While the topic is timely and relevant—especially given the critical need for reliable uncertainty estimates in clinical decision-making—the presentation suffers from several significant issues that affect clarity, technical rigor, and overall scientific contribution. The abstract and introduction suggest a novel integration of variational inference for capturing both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, but the methodological details are underdeveloped and at times confusing, particularly in the description of network architecture and training procedure. Key equations are referenced (e.g., Equation 1 through 9), yet the absence of actual mathematical expressions or sufficient verbal explanation impairs reproducibility and understanding. Furthermore, while the paper cites foundational works such as Gal & Ghahramani (2015) and Kendall & Gal (2017), it fails to clearly differentiate its approach from existing methods—especially in how it jointly models both types of uncertainty within a variational autoencoder-like framework—and does not adequately justify architectural choices. Basic concepts such as the reparameterization trick and ELBO are explained at a level suitable for an introductory tutorial, suggesting a lack of concision and focus for a conference venue like ICLR, where technical depth is expected. Evaluation is mentioned in terms of DSC and IoU, but without baseline comparisons or ablation on uncertainty calibration (e.g., via reliability diagrams or uncertainty-sensitivity analysis), claims about performance and robustness remain unsubstantiated. Most critically, the work currently lacks experimental validation of the uncertainty estimates themselves—measures such as predictive entropy, mutual information, or variance maps—which are essential for supporting the paper's central claims. To strengthen the paper, the authors must clarify the model formulation, provide rigorous experimental evaluation of uncertainty quality (not just segmentation accuracy), and better position their contribution relative to the state of the art in Bayesian deep learning for medical imaging.

comments.title: Incorrect decomposition claim in variational inference section
comments.quote: The above equation can be decomposed into two parts one of which is the KL divergence between the exact posterior and its variational approximation which needs to be minimized and the second is ELBO term which needs to be maximized. This is shown in Equation 8.
comments.explanation: Initially, I expected the decomposition in variational inference to follow the standard identity: log p(D) = ELBO + KL(q||p), where the KL term is between the variational approximation q and the true posterior p(w|D). However, the text claims that the KL divergence is between the 'exact posterior and its variational approximation', which would be KL(p||q), but ELBO derivation relies on KL(q||p), not KL(p||q). The KL divergence is not symmetric, so this is potentially a major error. While Equation 8 is not visible in the provided context, the verbal description suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of the decomposition. In standard treatments (e.g., Bishop, PRML; Kingma & Welling, 2013), it’s the KL(qθ(w)||p(w|D)) that appears, and minimizing this KL term is equivalent to maximizing ELBO. The phrasing 'KL divergence between the exact posterior and its variational approximation' strongly suggests KL(p||q), which is not computable and not used in VI. The surrounding context does not resolve this — if this is how the authors wrote it, it is misleading and could mislead a less experienced reader about the foundations of variational inference.

comments.title: Confusing and potentially incorrect claim about variance decomposition in uncertainty types
comments.quote: There are two types of uncertainty - aleatory and epistemic uncertainty where variance is the sum of both these.
comments.explanation: The statement that 'variance is the sum of both these' is ambiguous and risks being incorrect. In standard Bayesian deep learning literature (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017), for regression, the predictive variance can be decomposed as Var(y) = Ep[Var(y|w)] + Varp[E(y|w)], where the first term is aleatoric (data noise) and the second is epistemic (model uncertainty). However, in segmentation (a classification task), predictive uncertainty is typically measured via entropy or mutual information, not variance. The term 'variance' here is ill-defined for segmentation outputs. Moreover, even if interpreted in logit space, simply stating 'variance is the sum' without qualification oversimplifies and could mislead. The claim appears again in context near Equation 10 and 11 without any mathematical formulation. While the authors are likely trying to convey the idea of decomposing uncertainty sources, using 'variance' in a classification problem without clarification is technically problematic and may mislead readers about how uncertainty is quantified in their model.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'aleatoric' vs. 'aleatory'
comments.quote: There are two types of uncertainty - aleatory and epistemic uncertainty
comments.explanation: The passage uses the spelling 'aleatory', while throughout the rest of the paper (e.g., in section 3.6 and figure captions) it uses 'aleatoric'. This inconsistency could stem from OCR errors, but given that 'aleatoric' is the overwhelmingly standard term in the ML literature (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017; reference [25] uses 'aleatoric'), while 'aleatory' is common in philosophy/statistics but rare in ML, the inconsistency is notable. The context resolves this partially: later sections consistently use 'aleatoric', suggesting the authors’ intended term. Since multiple instances exist and the OCR does not appear to be at fault for this particular term (no garbled characters), it is likely a spelling inconsistency in the original. While not a technical error, it’s a terminology inconsistency that could confuse readers unfamiliar with the equivalence of the terms.

comments.title: Unclear definition of encoder and decoder functions in algorithm setup
comments.quote: Let dataset be denoted by _D_ : _{_ ( _xi, yi_ ) _}[N] i_ =1[, variational approximation of the posterior distribution] by _qθ_ ( _w_ ), encoder as _rψ_ ( _z|w_ ) and decoder as _pφ_ ( _w_ ).
comments.explanation: The notation here is confusing. In standard VAE nomenclature (Kingma & Welling, 2013), the encoder is qφ(z|x) (posterior over latent variables given input), and the decoder is pθ(x|z). But here, the encoder is defined as rψ(z|w), conditioning on weights w, and the decoder as pφ(w), which reconstructs weights without conditioning on latent z. This breaks the standard VAE flow. Moreover, in the context of Bayesian neural networks, the latent variable is typically over weights or activations, not over weights themselves as reconstruction targets. The algorithm later samples w and z and uses pφ(w|z), suggesting the decoder should be pφ(w|z), not pφ(w). The quoted line is missing a conditioning variable — it should likely be _pφ_ ( _w|z_ ). Given that the algorithm uses _pφ_ ( _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _|z_ )), the text definition is underspecified and inconsistent with the algorithm. Without clarification, it is not reproducible whether the decoder is unconditional or conditional, which is a critical architectural detail.

comments.title: Missing minus sign in ELBO objective in Equation 5
comments.explanation: Equation 5 as written implies minimizing the KL divergence, which is correct in principle, but in variational inference, this is intractable due to p(w|D). The authors go on to say that maximizing ELBO is equivalent. However, the standard ELBO is L = E_q[log p(w,D)] - KL(q||p(w)), so minimizing KL(q||p(w|D)) = -log p(D) + L. But the equation as written lacks proper syntax: it has an extra parenthesis and misses the fact that minimizing KL requires maximizing ELBO. More importantly, the expression is not the ELBO but the KL term. The confusion arises because the sentence just before says 'ELBO consists of sum of two terms: KL divergence... and negative log-likelihood', but this is incorrect: ELBO = -KL(q||p) + E_q[log p(D,w)], so it includes a negative KL term. The quoted line labels the KL minimization as Equation 5, but the actual ELBO is not yet defined. The subsequent text says ELBO 'consists of sum of two terms KL divergence and NLL', which is backwards — KL appears with a negative sign. This misrepresentation risks giving the impression that higher KL improves the objective, when in fact it harms it. While experts would correct this mentally, it constitutes a conceptual error in exposition.

comments.title: Misattribution of Monte Carlo dropout paper
comments.quote: Monte Carlo dropout networks (Kingma et al., 2015) use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes
comments.explanation: The citation attributes Monte Carlo dropout to Kingma et al. (2015), but the foundational work on using dropout for Bayesian inference is by Gal and Ghahramani (2015, 2016), who proved that dropout is a variational approximation. Kingma et al. (2015) did work on variational dropout (tied to the local reparameterization trick), but not on MC dropout at test time. While Kingma et al. (2015) is cited correctly in the reference list for 'Variational dropout and the local reparameterization trick', the text incorrectly applies it to MC dropout. The correct attribution for MC dropout in Bayesian deep learning is Gal & Ghahramani (2015), which is cited later. This is a citation error that misrepresents the intellectual contribution. The context does not resolve this — the sentence is standalone and incorrect. It undermines academic precision, though the technical content may still be sound.

comments.title: Ambiguous decomposition of variance into aleatoric and epistemic components
comments.quote: The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement appears to reference Equation 10 and 11 from the PAPER SUMMARY, which define the estimated mean and variance as $ \hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T \mu_t $ and $ \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T (\sigma_t^2 + \mu_t^2) - \hat{\mu}^2 $. Expanding the latter yields $ \hat{\sigma}^2 = \left(\frac{1}{T}\sum\sigma_t^2\right) + \left(\frac{1}{T}\sum\mu_t^2 - \hat{\mu}^2\right) $, where the first group is often interpreted as average predictive variance (aleatoric) and the second as mean prediction variance (epistemic). However, the passage does not clarify that this decomposition is heuristic and model-dependent. In standard Bayesian practice (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017), aleatoric uncertainty is captured via output distribution parameterization (e.g., learned noise), while epistemic comes from weight sampling — not directly from splitting $ \hat{\sigma}^2 $ as implied. The authors seem to conflate post-hoc variance decomposition with principled uncertainty modeling. While they correctly cite Kendall & Gal (2017), the text oversimplifies how these uncertainties are actually modeled, risking misinterpretation by readers unfamiliar with the distinction between modeling and post-processing.

comments.title: Incorrect objective function in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Compute stochastic gradients of the objective:     _L_[approx] =[] _i_  _−_ log _qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][] _−_ log _rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][] + log _pφ_ ( _i_ )  _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _|z_
comments.explanation: The stochastic objective in Algorithm 1 appears to be derived from a variational autoencoder framework: $ \mathbb{E}_q[\log p(w|z)] - \mathrm{KL}(q_\theta(w) \| p(w)) - \mathbb{E}_q[\mathrm{KL}(r_\psi(z|w) \| p(z))] $. However, in the context of Bayesian neural networks, the ELBO should include a data-fidelity term (i.e., $ \log p(D|w) $) as per Eq 4 and Eq 9 in the PAPER SUMMARY. The current formulation omits any dependence on the data labels $ y_i $, making it impossible to learn a meaningful posterior over weights for segmentation. Instead, it appears to optimize a prior-posterior alignment in latent space without reference to task performance. While the network architecture may be inspired by VAEs, the method is applied to supervised segmentation, hence the objective must depend on $ D $. This is a major flaw: without the likelihood term $ \log p(D|w) $, the algorithm does not perform supervised learning. The authors likely intended to include this term based on Eq 13 (forward referenced) but failed to write it explicitly here, rendering Algorithm 1 underspecified and theoretically invalid for its stated goal.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed input source and network design
comments.quote: Our model uses a similar encoder decoder architecture as that used in VAEs with the input to the encoder coming from a pre trained image segmentation architecture.
comments.explanation: The authors describe using outputs from pretrained models (e.g., U-Net) as input to their encoder. This suggests they are using the predictions of another segmentation network as input — however, this would imply processing segmentation maps (probability or label maps) rather than raw images. But in Figure 2 (not shown in text), and considering standard practice, the input to any segmentation network should be the MRI scan itself. Furthermore, the model is trained end-to-end with gradient descent, as stated later, contradicting the idea of fixed pretrained backbones. While multitask or distillation setups exist, the authors do not clarify whether the 'input' refers to features or logits from another network, or if this is meant to describe transfer learning. They correctly adopt an encoder-decoder structure like VAEs, but the description introduces confusion about what data the model actually consumes. Without clarification, readers may assume a feature-combination architecture that is not standard, leading to reproducibility issues.

comments.title: Undefined reparameterization sampling and missing Monte Carlo averaging in prediction
comments.quote: Sample weights with reparametrization: _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][]  Sample latent variables with reparametrization: _z_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][]
comments.explanation: The algorithm describes sampling both weights $ w $ and latent variables $ z $, suggesting a hierarchical Bayesian model. However, during inference, uncertainty quantification requires multiple stochastic forward passes (Monte Carlo sampling) to compute output variance — a standard step in BNNs. While MC dropout and deep ensembles are mentioned later, Algorithm 1 does not include any loop over test-time samples, nor does it describe how final predictions (mean/variance) are aggregated. The forward pass computes gradients, but the prediction pipeline itself is underspecified. The text mentions Equation 10 and 11 for estimating mean and variance, but does not specify whether these are computed from weight samples, latent samples, or both. Given that both $ w $ and $ z $ are stochastic, the propagation of uncertainty through both levels must be clearly defined. Without specifying how many samples are used or at which level uncertainty is measured, the method lacks reproducibility.

comments.title: Uncertainty Calculation in Deep Ensembles Uses Entropy of Averaged Predictions, Not Variance
comments.quote: **2. Deep ensembles:** We collect the results of each image trained on five models and compute the average mean. We then calculate the entropy of these mean values as the uncertainty value on each image.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the use of entropy on the average predictions of deep ensembles seems plausible, as predictive entropy is a standard uncertainty measure in Bayesian classification. The authors likely intend to quantify uncertainty via the entropy of the averaged softmax outputs across models, which is a valid approach: $ \text{Uncertainty} = -\sum_c \bar{p}_c \log \bar{p}_c $, where $ \bar{p}_c $ is the mean predictive probability for class $ c $. However, the phrasing is misleading. Saying they 'compute the average mean' and then 'calculate the entropy of these mean values' suggests they are taking entropy over the *means* as if they were logits or scalars, which would not make sense. More precisely, they should clarify that they compute the ensemble mean of class probabilities and then take entropy of that distribution. While this is common practice, the current wording could mislead a reader into thinking uncertainty is derived from variability *in* the means rather than from the uncertainty *of* the mean prediction. Context from standard practice in the field supports that this is likely just awkward phrasing, not a technical error.

comments.title: Ensemble MC Dropout Equation Missing from Text
comments.quote:
This can be defined using the below equation:

where _D_ presents how many models are there in ensemble MC dropout, _k_ , _i_ , _j_ are the indexes of _D_ , _M_ , _c_ and other variables same as MC dropout uncertainty formula above.
comments.explanation: The text promises a formal equation for the 'Ensemble MC Dropout' method but fails to provide it. Instead, it only describes index variables and references prior definitions. The uncertainty in MC dropout is typically $ \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^M p^{(m)}(1 - p^{(m)}) $ (for binary case) or predictive entropy in multiclass. For ensemble MC dropout, one would expect either the mean over ensemble members of such per-model uncertainty, or a two-level average over models and MC samples. Given that the method combines deep ensembles and MC dropout, the expectation is a double average: over $ D $ models and $ M $ dropout samples per model. Without the explicit formula, it's impossible to verify reproducibility. The reference to 'other variables same as MC dropout' is too vague—MC dropout uncertainty uses $ p_i^{(m)} $, but it's unclear whether the ensemble version averages probabilities first or computes uncertainty per model. This is a methodological gap, not just a formatting issue.

comments.title: Table Reference Conflict: Table 2 Used for Two Different Purposes
comments.quote:
Table 2: Uncertainty mean value of samples in the validation/test sets by our network.

The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2.
comments.explanation: This is a critical inconsistency. The first table titled 'Table 2' presents uncertainty values for three models (MC dropout, deep ensembles, etc.) across validation and test sets. Later, the text states that 'The Mean Dice Similarity value [...] are shown in Table 2', yet it refers to a different dataset — DSC values at different training sizes — which is actually labeled 'Table 3' in the following lines. The immediate issue is that the sentence incorrectly cites 'Table 2' when it should cite 'Table 3'. This is more than a typo: it creates confusion about which results are being reported. A careful reader might initially think Table 2 contains multiple types of results, but the actual table immediately after the claim is Table 3 (DSC) and then Table 4 (IoU). This misattribution risks misreading the results and undermines clarity. Even if OCR caused formatting shifts, the sentence structure assigns two distinct contents to Table 2. This could mislead readers about what the reported uncertainty values correspond to.

comments.title: Claimed Superiority to State-of-the-Art Lacks Supporting Evidence
comments.quote: We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.
comments.explanation: This claim is problematic because no comparison to prior work is shown in the provided context. Tables 3 and 4 only report DSC and IoU values for different training set sizes (e.g. 5, 10, 15, 20 patients), not against published methods on BRATS. Without reference to baseline models (e.g., standard U-Net, prior uncertainty-aware models like Kendall & Gal 2017), there is no evidence in the text to support 'outperforming previous state of the art'. The highest DSC reported is 64.3 (with 20 training samples), which is far below typical BRATS performance (often >80 for whole tumor), suggesting the 'train size' may refer to a small ablation setting, not full data. The authors may have made this comparison in a section not included in the context, but based on what is here, the claim is unjustified and overreaching.

comments.title: Aleatoric and Epistemic Uncertainties Described as Inversely Correlated Without Justification
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The text claims a correlation between aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty, specifically implying an inverse relationship: higher confidence in one leads to higher uncertainty in the other. This is counterintuitive and unexplained. Standard understanding (e.g., Kendall & Gal 2017) treats them as conceptually distinct—epistemic decreases with data, aleatoric does not—and they are often positively correlated in ambiguous regions (e.g., tumor boundaries). The statement suggests a trade-off not supported by standard models. While Figure 4 may visually suggest this, the lack of quantitative evidence or theoretical justification makes this claim speculative. The phrasing 'co-related' (sic) lacks precision, and no metric or analysis is offered. This risks misleading readers about the nature of uncertainty decomposition.

comments.title: Inconsistent ensemble MC dropout uncertainty formula in text vs. definition
comments.quote: Ensemble MC dropout uncertainty: $ \text{Uncertainty} = \frac{1}{D} \sum_{k=1}^D \left[ \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^M p_i^{(k,m)} (1 - p_i^{(k,m)}) \right] $, where $ D $ is the number of models and $ p_i^{(k,m)} $ is the predicted probability from the $ k $-th model and $ m $-th Monte Carlo sample.
comments.explanation: The equation defines uncertainty using a Bernoulli variance form $ p(1-p) $, which suggests it's computing expected variance per prediction across Monte Carlo samples and models. However, the surrounding prose (section 4) describes uncertainty for ensemble MC dropout as derived by 'averaging across five models' in a manner similar to MC dropout. The issue is that the formula shown computes the average of the variances (i.e. average of $ p(1-p) $), not the full decomposition of uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic components as used in standard Bayesian deep learning (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017). Moreover, the index $ i $ is used both as a data point index in the dataset earlier, but here appears as the class or pixel index — unclear what is being summed over. While this might represent per-pixel uncertainty averaged over MC samples and models, the use of $ p(1-p) $ alone collapses both types of uncertainty and does not separate them, contradicting the paper’s emphasis on distinguishing aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty (section 2.4). Additionally, the entropy-based uncertainty mentioned in the **Notation & Definitions** section (entropy of averaged predictions) is inconsistent with this $ p(1-p) $-based formula. The two definitions — entropy of mean predictions (for deep ensembles) and average Bernoulli variance (for ensemble MC dropout) — are methodologically different and should be reconciled. Even if this is intentional, the lack of justification for using a different uncertainty quantification scheme across methods makes comparisons in Table 2 questionable. Given that uncertainty values are compared across methods, using incompatible measures undermines the validity of the key claim that ensemble MC dropout yields 'higher uncertainty' as a sign of better sensitivity.

comments.title: Misleading claim about inverse correlation between aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The claim states that higher confidence (lower uncertainty) in one type leads to higher uncertainty in the other — i.e., an inverse relationship. However, this misrepresents standard understanding in Bayesian deep learning. Aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty capture different sources: data noise vs. model uncertainty. They are not inherently inversely correlated. For instance, ambiguous regions (e.g., tumor boundaries) often exhibit high values in both. The authors likely observed a qualitative spatial complementarity in their visualizations (e.g., bright regions in one map appear dull in the other), but inferring a general inverse correlation across the model is unjustified without statistical evidence (e.g., pixel-wise correlation analysis). The **Key Claims** summary similarly states they are 'inversely correlated', amplifying the overstatement. While such patterns may arise in specific cases due to model behavior or dataset characteristics, claiming a systematic inverse relationship goes beyond what is typically supported in the literature and could mislead readers about the nature of uncertainty decomposition.

comments.title: Table numbering conflict and mismatched content
comments.quote: The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2.\n\nThe IOU value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 3.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Table 2' and 'Table 3' as containing DSC and IoU metrics across train sizes, respectively. However, the actual Table 2 is labeled as 'Uncertainty mean value of samples...' and Table 3 is 'Mean Dice Similarity metrics...'. So the reference to 'Table 2' for DSC values is incorrect — it should be Table 3. Similarly, referencing 'Table 3' for IoU is wrong — the IoU table is Table 4. This confusion likely arose because the uncertainty table (Table 2) appears earlier. While the tables themselves contain reasonable data, this cross-reference error could cause confusion about which results support which claim. It affects reproducibility and clarity, especially since the key claim about performance improving with training size depends on these tables. The DSC and IoU results (Tables 3 and 4) do support the claim about increasing performance with train size, so the data is sound — only the textual references are swapped.

comments.title: Incoherent algorithm description and notation
comments.quote: Sample weights with reparametrization: _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][]  Sample latent variables with reparametrization: _z_[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][] Compute stochastic gradients of the objective:     _L_[approx] =[] _i_  _−_ log _qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][] _−_ log _rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][] + log _pφ_ ( _i_ )  _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _|z_
comments.explanation: The OCR has severely distorted this section of Algorithm 1, making it nearly unreadable. Symbols like  and [] are clearly artifacts. While OCR errors are not the authors’ fault *per se*, the presence of uncorrected or non-standard notation makes the algorithm irreproducible. The intended form likely follows standard variational inference: sampling weights via reparameterization from the variational posterior, computing the ELBO as a combination of log-likelihood, prior, and posterior terms. However, the current rendering lacks proper summations, variable scoping, and equation structure. Since this is the only presentation of the training algorithm, and the model is central to the paper, the underspecification due to OCR corruption is a problem — a reader cannot reproduce the method. The core idea (VAE-like encoder-decoder with weight distributions) is clear from surrounding text, but the actual step-by-step procedure is lost. This would be a 'minor' issue if the method were described elsewhere, but the algorithm is the main procedural account.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty decomposition
comments.quote: The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: This statement in section 2.4 follows Equation 11 (not shown in full), but no formal equation is provided for the decomposition. In standard Bayesian semantic segmentation (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017), aleatoric uncertainty is captured via learned homoscedastic or heteroscedastic noise, while epistemic uncertainty is estimated via MC dropout or sampling from the weight posterior. The paper alludes to this but does not specify how the two components are computed in their model. Are they decomposing the predictive variance into mean-of-squares vs. square-of-means (as in standard MC dropout)? If so, that should be stated. The claim that these are inversely correlated rests on this decomposition being correctly implemented, but without equations or algorithmic details, it's impossible to verify. The **Key Paper Summary** states the entropy of averaged predictions is used, but that conflates both sources and does not allow separation. The lack of formal definitions undermines the interpretability of Figure 4 and the claims in section 4 about the relationship between the two uncertainty types.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The submitted manuscript provides a clear motivation for the importance of uncertainty quantification in medical imaging, effectively situating its contribution within the established field of Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs). The review of relevant literature, ranging from U-Net architectures to variational inference (VI) and the decomposition of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty, demonstrates a solid grasp of the theoretical landscape. However, the paper’s primary weakness at this stage is its reliance on well-trodden ground; the introduction and related work sections cover foundational concepts extensively without clearly delineating the "novelty" of this specific implementation. Furthermore, the writing suffers from minor technical inaccuracies and grammatical inconsistencies (e.g., misattributions of specific papers to entire sub-fields, and vague descriptions of "scalability" issues). To improve the paper's quality, the authors must transition from a general review of Bayesian methods to a precise description of their specific architecture, emphasizing why their variation of VI is uniquely suited for the BRAST dataset and how it overcomes the scalability challenges cited previously.

comments.title: Incorrect and non-standard mathematical formulation of ELBO
comments.quote:
ELBO consists of sum of two terms Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between two distributions and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) as defined in Equation 5.
min KL ( qθ ( w )) ∥p ( w|D )) (5)
comments.explanation: Equation 5 and the associated text present a significant conceptual error. The ELBO is defined as the objective to be *maximized*, and it is mathematically equivalent to the Expected Log-Likelihood minus the KL divergence between the variational posterior and the prior. The authors equate the minimization of KL divergence to the ELBO, and the notation in equation 5 (using a double-bar operator that is not standard for KL divergence) is mathematically incoherent. Furthermore, minimizing KL(q||p(w|data)) is equivalent to maximizing the log-marginal likelihood, but the ELBO is a lower bound on that log-marginal likelihood. Stating the ELBO *consists of* the sum of KL and NLL is factually wrong; the standard derivation results in a difference (Log-likelihood - KL).

comments.title: Unclear relation between variance and uncertainty types
comments.quote: The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The text references Equations 10 and 11 to support this claim, but the equations themselves are not provided in the snippet. However, the claim is a strong one for which the paper provides no underlying derivation or reference to specific components of the variance (e.g., the predictive variance decomposition). Without the context of the variance formula being discussed, the text risks being misleading by making a blanket claim about 'the first term' vs 'the second term' without defining the decomposition.

comments.title: Inconsistent formulation of ALEATORIC/EPISTEMIC uncertainty update
comments.quote: it with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The text claims that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are correlated in an inverse manner (if one increases, the other decreases). In standard BNN literature (e.g., Kendall and Gal, 2017), these two types of uncertainty are considered additive components of the total predictive variance, arising from different sources (data noise vs. model parameter ignorance). There is no theoretical basis provided for why they must be negatively correlated, nor does this align with the typical behavior of these metrics. This claim is confusing and lacks empirical or theoretical justification.

comments.title: Incorrect statement regarding the correlation of uncertainty types
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are inherently correlated and move in opposition. In standard Bayesian DL literature (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017), these quantities are treated as distinct components of the total predictive variance (aleatoric as data-dependent observation noise and epistemic as model-parameter uncertainty). The claim that they 'co-relate' or increase/decrease in opposition is a strong, non-standard statement that lacks empirical justification or reference to a specific mechanism (like training dynamics causing a trade-off). This is confusing and potentially misleading for a reader trying to interpret the results.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses a highly relevant topic but currently reads more like an introductory survey than a novel research contribution, which significantly limits its suitability for a top-tier venue like ICLR. While the motivation for uncertainty-aware biomedical segmentation is sound, the paper lacks methodological innovation, appearing to apply standard variational inference (e.g., mean-field approximations or Bayes-by-Backprop) to a conventional U-Net without architectural adaptations, theoretical insights, or principled handling of the unique challenges posed by medical imaging data. Clarity and scholarly rigor are undermined by several technical inaccuracies and citation misattributions, such as conflating Monte Carlo dropout with deep Gaussian processes, misattributing the variational autoencoder and local reparameterization trick primarily to Kingma et al. (2015) without acknowledging foundational contributions, and presenting well-known ELBO and uncertainty decomposition derivations without critically linking them to the proposed approach. Moreover, the abstract claims quantitative results on the BRATS dataset using DSC and IoU, yet the provided text contains no methodology, experimental setup, ablation studies, or comparisons to strong baselines (e.g., MC dropout, deep ensembles, or deterministic U-Nets), making it impossible to assess the validity of the claims. To meet ICLR standards, the paper must present a clearly defined novel architecture or inference strategy, provide rigorous empirical validation with statistical significance and uncertainty calibration metrics (e.g., ECE, reliability diagrams), and situate its contributions within a sharper critical synthesis of prior work that explicitly identifies what gaps this approach fills beyond straightforward application of existing Bayesian deep learning tools.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for Monte Carlo Dropout
comments.quote: Monte Carlo dropout networks (Kingma et al., 2015) use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes which still lack theoretical understanding.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute Monte Carlo Dropout to Kingma et al. (2015), but the seminal work introducing MC Dropout as a Bayesian approximation is Gal and Ghahramani (2015/2016). Kingma et al. (2015) correctly corresponds to the Variational Autoencoder and the Local Reparameterization Trick (also cited later in the same paragraph), but is misapplied here. This is a factual literature attribution error that misidentifies the origin of a core technique, though it does not invalidate the methodological description itself.

comments.title: Text-formula mismatch in ELBO definition
comments.quote:
ELBO consists of sum of two terms Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between two distributions and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) as defined in Equation 5.

min KL ( _qθ_ ( _w_ )) _∥p_ ( _w|D_ )) (5)
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that the ELBO comprises two terms: the KL divergence and the negative log-likelihood. However, Equation 5 only presents a minimization objective for the KL divergence between the variational posterior q_\theta(w) and the true posterior p(w|D). The expected log-likelihood (or data fit) term is entirely absent. In variational inference, the ELBO is formulated as E[log p(x|z)] - KL(q||p), and minimizing the KL divergence to the true posterior is mathematically equivalent to maximizing the ELBO. The authors conflate the optimization objective with the components of the ELBO, making the equation not match the textual definition provided.

comments.title: Misleading characterization of epistemic uncertainty
comments.quote: Epistemic uncertainty on the other hand is a measure of the variability of predictions from the model which is tied to various metrics used for evaluation like accuracy, loss etc.
comments.explanation: The claim that epistemic uncertainty is 'tied to various metrics used for evaluation like accuracy, loss etc.' is conceptually inaccurate. Epistemic uncertainty quantifies model ignorance due to limited or unrepresentative training data and is a property of the Bayesian posterior predictive distribution (Kendall & Gal, 2017). It is fundamentally independent of post-hoc evaluation metrics like accuracy or loss. While the subsequent clause correctly notes it represents 'uncertainty inside the model', the linkage to evaluation metrics is misleading and conflates predictive uncertainty with model performance scores.

comments.title: Mismatched table references in Results section
comments.quote:
The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2.
The IOU value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 3.
comments.explanation: The prose explicitly states that Table 2 contains Mean Dice values and Table 3 contains IoU values. However, upon inspecting the actual tables, Table 2 is captioned 'Uncertainty mean value...' and lists uncertainty metrics, while Table 3 actually contains the Dice scores and Table 4 contains IoU. This direct cross-referencing error contradicts the printed tables and misleads readers trying to locate the primary segmentation performance results. The authors likely rearranged tables during drafting without updating the cross-references. This is a substantive reporting error easily fixed by aligning the text references with the actual table labels.

comments.title: Questionable dependency claim for aleatoric uncertainty
comments.quote: It is highly dependent on bias and distribution inside the input data but not the size of training samples.
comments.explanation: The claim that aleatoric uncertainty is 'highly dependent on bias' contradicts the foundational separation between aleatoric (data-driven) and epistemic (model-driven) components. In Bayesian uncertainty decomposition, aleatoric uncertainty captures irreducible noise inherent in the data-generating process (e.g., sensor noise, label ambiguity). By definition, it is independent of the model's learned parameters, including bias terms. While the underlying data distribution influences the observed aleatoric uncertainty, tying it to model bias is conceptually incorrect.

comments.title: Underspecified methodology: framework conflation, missing likelihood, and absent reparameterization math
comments.quote:
The objective function used in this work is defined in Equation 13: ... Compute stochastic gradients of the objective: ... _L_[approx] =[] _i_  _−_ log _qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][] _−_ log _rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][] + log _pφ_ ( _i_ )  _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _|z_ 

Sample weights with reparametrization: w[(] _[i]_[)] _∼ qθi_ _w_[(] _[i]_[)][]
comments.explanation: The notation in Equation 13 mixes Bayesian Neural Network (BNN) and Variational Autoencoder (VAE) frameworks non-standardly. The authors define $q_\theta(w)$ as the variational posterior over weights, yet introduce an encoder $r_\psi(z|w)$ and decoder $p_\phi(w)$ for latent variables $z$. More critically, the stated approximate objective completely omits the data reconstruction likelihood term $\log p(D|w)$, making the objective appear unsupervised despite Section 3.5 introducing a BCE+Dice loss. Additionally, Algorithm 1 claims stochastic sampling 'with reparametrization' but lacks the deterministic transformation equation (typically $w = \mu + \epsilon \odot \sigma$) and omits the Local Reparameterization Trick implementation. The optimization procedure is also underspecified, as $\phi$ is updated via an unclear aggregation rule rather than gradient descent. These omissions fundamentally hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified uncertainty estimation methods
comments.quote:
**2. Deep ensembles:** We collect the results of each image trained on five models and compute the average mean. We then calculate the entropy of these mean values as the uncertainty value on each image.

**3. Ensemble MC dropout:** This method is a combination of MC dropout and deep ensembles. It contains 5 Bayesian Neural Networks and thus following a similar way to compute MC dropout but with the average value of these five models. This can be defined using the below equation:

where _D_ presents how many models are there in ensemble MC dropout, _k_ , _i_ , _j_ are the indexes of _D_ , _M_ , _c_ and other variables same as MC dropout uncertainty formula above.
comments.explanation: The descriptions for deep ensembles and ensemble MC dropout are technically underspecified. For deep ensembles, computing 'the average mean' and then 'the entropy of these mean values' is mathematically ambiguous; uncertainty is typically derived from variance across predictions or entropy of the averaged probability distribution, not entropy of a single averaged mean vector. For ensemble MC dropout, the text references a missing/dropped equation and provides a vague procedural description. It remains unclear whether the MC sampling (M) and ensemble averaging (D) are nested in a specific order, and how reparameterization interfaces with the ensemble structure. These gaps hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Questionable claim on aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty correlation
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The claim that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are inversely 'co-related' contradicts standard Bayesian deep learning literature (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017), where these uncertainties are treated as additive variance components. Epistemic uncertainty stems from model parameter uncertainty and decreases with more data, while aleatoric uncertainty remains fixed to the dataset's noise floor. The stated inverse relationship is presented without empirical evidence or theoretical derivation. If this is an idiosyncratic observation specific to their BRATS experiments, it requires explicit justification and supporting ablation plots; otherwise, it risks misleading readers about the fundamental nature of Bayesian uncertainty decomposition.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated state-of-the-art claim
comments.quote: We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that the proposed model 'outperform[s] previous state of the art', but the Results section contains no comparative baselines, SOTA reference values, or benchmark tables. The paper only reports its own performance across four very small training splits (sizes 5, 10, 15, 20), peaking at a DSC of 64.3% and IoU of 55.8%. Given that established methods on the BRATS benchmark routinely achieve DSC scores above 80-85%, this claim is empirically unfounded based on the provided data. It overstates the model's practical utility and misleads readers evaluating the paper's contribution.

It represents reducible uncertainty that decreases as more data is observed. It is not defined by or intrinsically tied to evaluation metrics like accuracy or loss. comments.quote: The objective function used in this work is defined in Equation 13: ... Compute stochastic gradients of the objective: ... _L_[approx] =[] _i_  _−_ log _qθi_  _w_[(] _[i]_[)][] _−_ log _rψ_ ( _i_ )  _z|w_ [(] _[i]_[)][] + log _pφ_ ( _i_ )  _w_[(] _[i]_[)] _|z_

comments.title: Underspecified uncertainty estimation methods
comments.quote:
**2. Deep ensembles:** We collect the results of each image trained on five models and compute the average mean. We then calculate the entropy of these mean values as the uncertainty value on each image.

**3. Ensemble MC dropout:** This method is a combination of MC dropout and deep ensembles. It contains 5 Bayesian Neural Networks and thus following a similar way to compute MC dropout but with the average value of these five models. This can be defined using the below equation:

where _D_ presents how many models are there in ensemble MC dropout, _k_ , _i_ , _j_ are the indexes of _D_ , _M_ , _c_ and other variables same as MC dropout uncertainty formula above.
comments.explanation: The descriptions for deep ensembles and ensemble MC dropout are technically underspecified. For deep ensembles, computing 'the average mean' and then 'the entropy of these mean values' is mathematically ambiguous. In standard practice, uncertainty is derived from the variance across ensemble predictions or the entropy of the averaged probability distribution; calculating entropy of a single averaged mean vector would not yield a per-image uncertainty measure. For ensemble MC dropout, the text references a missing equation (likely dropped during OCR/typesetting) and provides a vague procedural description. It remains unclear whether the MC sampling (M) and ensemble averaging (D) are nested in a specific order, and how the reparameterization trick interfaces with the ensemble structure. Given that uncertainty quantification hinges on precise probabilistic definitions, these gaps hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Questionable claim on aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty correlation
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: I was initially struck by the claim that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are inversely 'co-related'. I cross-referenced this with the paper's own Section 2.4 definitions and standard Bayesian deep learning literature (e.g., Kendall & Gal, 2017), where these uncertainties are treated as additive variance components: aleatoric stems from inherent data/observation noise, while epistemic stems from model parameter uncertainty. Conventionally, epistemic uncertainty decreases with more data or better training, while aleatoric uncertainty remains fixed to the dataset's noise floor. The stated inverse relationship contradicts this foundational understanding and is presented without empirical evidence or theoretical derivation. If this was an idiosyncratic observation specific to their BRATS experiments or architecture, it requires explicit justification and supporting ablation plots. Otherwise, it risks misleading readers about the fundamental nature of Bayesian uncertainty decomposition.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated state-of-the-art claim

The paper only reports its own performance across four very small training splits (sizes 5, 10, 15, 20), peaking at a DSC of 64.3% and IoU of 55.8%. Given that established methods on the BRATS benchmark routinely achieve DSC scores above 80-85%, this claim is empirically unfounded based on the provided data.
